coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots triggered the induction of ROS-mediated cytoprotective autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ting
Qu, Jing
Yao, Ying
Zhang, Yitenng
Ma, Ying
Wu, Daming
Cao, Yuna
Yang, Mengran
Zhang, Yuanjian
Tang, Meng
Pu, Yuepu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging fluorescent nano-imaging probe due to their unique characteristics, such as good conductivity, carbon-based chemical composition, and photochemical stability, which sets up the potential of outperforming the classic metal-based quantum dots (QDs). It is a timely effort to proactively investigate the biocompatibility feature of CDs with a view to safely utilize this emerging nanomaterial in biological systems. In this study, we assessed the safety profile of an in-house synthesized CDs in hepatocyte-like Hepa 1–6 cells, which represents an important target organ for CDs exposure through either particle uptake and/or accumulation and elimination from primary exposure sites post particle administration. We not only demonstrated a dose- and time-dependent compromised cell viability, but also observed the induction of autophagy at high concentration (i.e. 400 μg mL-1), authenticated by the conversion of microtubule-associated protein light chain 3 (LC3)-I to LC3-II. We attributed these changes as the protective mechanism by which the cells used to compensate for CDs-induced apoptosis and cytotoxicity. The involvement of autophagy was further confirmed because the cytotoxicity profile can be increased or reduced by the use of 3-MA (autophagy inhibitor) and NAC (ROS inhibitor), respectively. Collectively, our findings revealed dose-dependent moderate cytotoxicity in Hepa 1–6 cells. Mechanistic understanding of autophagy during the cellular process revealed the homeostasis when liver cells deal with CDs as an external insult.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
ROS
Autophagy
Apoptosis
Liver toxicity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and treatment with CDs
Cellular ultrastructural visualization under transmission electron microscopy
Cellular uptake, distribution and cytotoxicity of CDs
Measurements of apoptosis in Hepa1-6 cells treated CDs
ROS production and its relationship with cellular uptake
CDs induced autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells and the role of autophagy in CDs-induced toxicity
The role of ROS generation on CDs triggered autophagy

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National College Students Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of synthesized CDs. (A) TEM micrograph of CDs. (B) Size distributions measurements of the CDs. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CDs. (D) Scan XPS spectra of CDs. (E) FTIR analysis of CDs.

Cellular uptake, subcellular localization and Cytotoxicity screening of CDs in Hepa 1–6 cells. (A) Hepa1-6 cells were treated with CDs at 100, 400 μg mL-1 for 12 h. The results of side-scatter light of flow cytometry demonstrated that CDs were apparently engulfed by Hepa1-6 cells. (B) Hepa1-6 cells were treated with CDs for 12hr and were stained with organelle probes (Lyso-Tracker Red), the co-localizations of CDs and organelle probes were examined with confocal laser scanning microscopy. Conditions: blue fluorescence channel, Ex/Em: 405/420–500 nm for CDs; red fluorescence channel, Ex/Em: 559/570–640 nm for Lyso-Tracker Red. Use of an MTT (C) assay and LDH (D) assay to assess the viability of Hepa 1–6 cells after exposure to CDs for 24 h over a dose range of 0–400 μg mL-1. Changes in cellular morphology (E) following exposure to CDs.

CDs induce apoptosis in Hepa1-6 cells. (A) Cells were stained with annexin V-FITC/PI and apoptotic proportion was quantified by flow cytometry. (B) Presence of apoptotic cells was analyzed by Annexin-V staining using flow cytometry in CDs-treated Hepa1-6 cells after 24 h treatment. ∗P < 0.05 vs. control. (C) The protein expressions of Bax, Bcl-2 and Cleaved caspase-3 were detected in Western blot analysis and band intensities were quantified and normalized to GAPHD (D).

Detection of intracellular ROS levels and the production of ROS depends on the uptake of CDs by Hepa1-6 cells. (A) Fluorescence images of DCFH-DA staining after CDs treatment for 12 h and 24 h. (B) Fluorescence intensity after CDs treatment measured by flow cytometry at 24 h ∗P < 0.05 vs. control. (C) Contour plot patterns of SSC and DCF intensity after treatment with 100 and 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 4 h. (D) The population was divided into two groups according to SSC intensity, and the corresponding DCF intensity was expressed using colors, and SSC and DCF intensity were analyzed by FCM.

CDs trigger the autophagic response in Hepa1-6 cells. (A) Hepa1-6 cells were transiently transfected with eGFP-LC3 plasmid for 24 h and treated with 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 12 h. The green color eGFP-LC3 puncta were observed with confocal microscopy; (B) Quantification of eGFP-LC3 puncta formation with cells after treatments; (C) Hepa1-6 cells were treated with various concentration of CDs for 12 h and 24 h. The level of LC3-II and Beclin-1 expression was detected by were analyzed by Western blot, Band intensities were quantified and normalized to GAPDH (D and E). (F) TEM of Hepa1-6 cells treated with PBS (control) or 400 μg mL-1 for 12 h, arrows indicate autophagosomes and autolysosomes. ∗P < 0.05.

Suppression of autophagy decreased cytotoxicity of CDs in Hepa1-6 cells. (A) The Hepa1-6 cells were incubated with CDs with the presence or absence of the autophagy inhibitor 3-MA for 24 h. The LC3 and Cleaved caspase 3 were analyzed by Western blot, Band intensities were quantified and normalized to GAPHD (B and C). The Hepa1-6 cells were incubated with 0, 100 and 400 μg mL-1 of CDs after 24 h with the presence or absence of 3-MA, then the presence of apoptotic cells were stained with Annexin V/PI and analyzed by flow cytometry (D); and the cell viability was analyzed by MTT. (E) ∗P < 0.05 versus control, #P < 0.05 versus CDs.

Intracellular ROS generation was involved in autophagy caused by CDs. (A) Hepa1-6 cells transfected with eGFP-LC3 were treated with CDs in the presence and absence of NAC. The green color eGFP-LC3 puncta were observed with confocal microscope T; (B) Quantification of eGFP-LC3 puncta formation with cells after treatments. (C) LC3 and Cleaved caspase-3 levels detected by Western blotting in the presence and absence of NAC and quantified by densitometric analysis relative to GAPDH. (D) The Hepa1-6 cells were incubated with 0, 100, 200 and 400 μg mL −1 of CDs in the presence or absence of antioxidant NAC for 24 h; the cell viability was analyzed by MTT. ∗P < 0.05 versus control, #P < 0.05 versus CDs.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots triggered the induction of ROS-mediated cytoprotective autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education, School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210009, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Medicine and Engineering, Ministry of Education; School of Public Health, Southeast University, Nanjing 210009, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory of Smart Carbon-Rich Materials and Devices, Jiangsu Province Hi-Tech Key Laboratory for Bio-Medical Research, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory of Smart Carbon-Rich Materials and Devices
Jiangsu Province Hi-Tech Key Laboratory for Bio-Medical Research
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Engineering Laboratory of Smart Carbon-Rich Materials and Devices, Jiangsu Province Hi-Tech Key Laboratory for Bio-Medical Research, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are an emerging fluorescent nano-imaging probe due to their unique characteristics, such as good conductivity, carbon-based chemical composition, and photochemical stability, which sets up the potential of outperforming the classic metal-based quantum dots (QDs). It is a timely effort to proactively investigate the biocompatibility feature of CDs with a view to safely utilize this emerging nanomaterial in biological systems. In this study, we assessed the safety profile of an in-house synthesized CDs in hepatocyte-like Hepa 1–6 cells, which represents an important target organ for CDs exposure through either particle uptake and/or accumulation and elimination from primary exposure sites post particle administration. We not only demonstrated a dose- and time-dependent compromised cell viability, but also observed the induction of autophagy at high concentration (i.e. 400 μg mL-1), authenticated by the conversion of microtubule-associated protein light chain 3 (LC3)-I to LC3-II. We attributed these changes as the protective mechanism by which the cells used to compensate for CDs-induced apoptosis and cytotoxicity. The involvement of autophagy was further confirmed because the cytotoxicity profile can be increased or reduced by the use of 3-MA (autophagy inhibitor) and NAC (ROS inhibitor), respectively. Collectively, our findings revealed dose-dependent moderate cytotoxicity in Hepa 1–6 cells. Mechanistic understanding of autophagy during the cellular process revealed the homeostasis when liver cells deal with CDs as an external insult.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum Dots (QDs), a class of promising nanomaterials, have attracted widespread attention for their serving as live cell imaging, the fluorescent probes, disease diagnosis as well as in vivo imaging due to the unique optical properties. Compared to organic dyes, QDs have unique photophysical properties including intensive fluorescence, photo-stability, high quantum yield and size-tunable light emission (Matea et al., 2017; Pohanka, 2017). However, since traditional semiconductor QDs (semi-QDs) frequently contain heavy metals such as Cd2+, the safety concern becomes a hamper for the wide utilization in the biological system (Han et al., 2019). At low concentration, semi-QDs may exert obvious toxicity including the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), the release of inflammatory factors, alteration of mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), and classification of DNA damage due to their small size effect and release of metal ions (Chen et al., 2012). This differs from fluorescent carbon dots (CDs), which is perceived as putatively lower toxic alternatives with great potential for biomedical applications, including fluorescent tags, biosensors, chemosensors and bio-imaging (Zheng et al., 2015). The increase in the use of CDs will consequently lead to their releasing to environment and, potentially increasing human exposure. Therefore, it is important to fully understand the adverse health effects and toxic effects of CDs. However, the full safety profile especially the sub-lethal effects of CDs requires experimental proven. While there are emerging evidence showing homeostasis machinery may be influenced by CDs, most studies are descriptive with limited exploration of the mechanism.

Homeostasis is an important characteristic of living organisms, and maintenance of cellular homeostasis is important for cell survival. Autophagy, a highly regulated process that degrades proteins and recycles cellular products or damaged organelles subsequently, plays a vital role in maintaining cell homeostasis (Ryter et al., 2013). In addition to the role of maintaining cellular homeostasis, autophagy functions as an adaptive and inducible response to cellular stress response caused by exposure to external chemical and physical agents. More recent studies have reported various types of nanoparticles (NPs) can affect autophagy such as carbon nanomaterials, mental and mental oxide NPs, semiconductor nanocrystals, and 2D materials (Zheng et al., 2016). In certain cases, NPs are capable of overstimulating autophagy process in cells, leading to autophagic cell death, or trigger cytoprotective autophagy and maintain cell homeostasis. In addition to performing housekeeping functions at a basic level, autophagy can also be activated as a cytoprotective pathway upon activation by triggers that represent some form of stress (Peynshaert et al., 2014). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) is a trigger for autophagy, and NPs have been demonstrated to produce intracellular ROS. Under normal physiological conditions, ROS production is in balance with their elimination by scavenger systems, however, excessive intracellular ROS could influence lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, ultimately lead to the imbalance of intracellular homeostasis and cell death. Recently, it was demonstrated that NPs can induce ROS and autophagy, which serve as to buffer and control the level of intracellular stress and reduce toxic effects in cells, or lead to death by apoptosis, and contribute to many diseases (Fang et al., 2017). With respect to CDs, we envisage that autophagy machinery is involved because the small-sized CDs interacted with the biological components of the cells and internalized into the cells. For one respect, these foreign substances entering the cells may affect the subcellular structure due to their own properties, for another, they are likely to be perceived as foreign or toxic substances and may stimulate activated of autophagy to enhance cell clearance. In particular, nanomaterials of different sizes and compositions have been shown to induce activation of the autophagy system, a major catabolic pathway that mediates the degradation of a large number of intracellular materials. Although activation of autophagy can lead to increased clearance, once over-activated, it is possible to induce autophagic damage or to be involved in the activation of cell death programs (Popp and Segatori, 2015). There is increasing evidence on the correlation between autophagy and NPs including nanoparticle-activated autophagy or inhibition of autophagy, such as developed an autophagy regulation strategy using FN-CDs from autophagy vesicles for efficient visualized tumor therapy (Li et al., 2018). However, it is unclear how CDs interacts with autophagy pathways in detail. So we focused on the safety of CDs and the effect and possible mechanism of autophagy induced by carbon dots on intracellular homeostasis changes.

Another important consideration is to understand CD’s effect in the liver – a target organ that was overlooked. Previous studies showed that the QDs inevitably enter the organism in a variety of ways and are mainly accumulated in the liver. Furthermore, by interacting with the cells of the liver, the QDs would probably affect the liver’s function and histopathology (Cho et al., 2007; Zhu et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2019). This consideration is very relevant to CDs because, after intravenous administration, the majority of CDs could be recognized and taken up by the liver (i.e. up to 60%), and clearance through the liver (Tao et al., 2012). While little is known in terms of how liver processes CDs, we surmise that multiple cell types, such as hepatocyte, macrophage, and endothelial cells, etc, are involved. For proof-of-principle, we decided to prioritize cellular study in hepatocytes because of its high abundance in the liver (∼80%) among liver cell populations. Moreover, the ultra-small CDs size (less than ∼10 nm) falls off from the phagocytosis size range (0.1–10 nm) and creates the possibility of hepatocytes access post CDs exposure (Shang et al., 2014).

Against this background, we hypothesize that CDs influence the homeostasis machinery in hepatocyte through an autophagy mediated process. Accordingly, we first evaluated the toxicity of CDs in liver cells with the focus on the changes in cellular homeostasis caused by CDs and alteration on autophagy biology. We found CDs were found to be localized in the lysosome observed and caused ROS production which correlated with intracellular uptake of CDs. We demonstrate that elevated ROS caused by intracellular uptake of CDs was likely an important autophagy inducer and autophagy acts as a protective defender in CDs-induced cytotoxicity and maintaining cell homeostasis, these findings provide valuable insights into the mechanisms by which CDs induces cell death in Hepa1-6 cells.

The CDs are synthesized by a facile one-pot two-step strategy as our previously described (Yang et al., 2019). Briefly, DCDA was added into the muffle furnace, ramping for 1 h and heating for 4 h at 450 °C to get CN450. After ground into powder using agate mortar, the obtained yellow-colored carbon nitride was put into the center of the furnace and heated to 680 °C under ultra-pure N2 flow (16 L min-1, 20 °C, 1 atm) within 1 h, and kept at this temperature for 4 h. Then, the mixture vapors of various CN nano-fragments along with carbon/nitrogen-containing small gas molecules were guided and collected by 50 mL of DI H2O to obtain a yellow color dispersion. After 30 min ultrasonic stirring, large solid precipitation was removed from the dispersion via centrifuging at 13,000 rpm for 30 min. After further dialysis (Cut-off molecular weight: 3000 Da, Shanghai yuanye Bio-Technology Co, Ltd) for 24 h in water, the purified CNDs outside the dialysis bag were collected and presented as CN450(1 + 4)-H2O. This sample derivative was designated CDs. Several analytical techniques have been used to characterize the synthesized CDs. The morphology and primary sizes of synthesized CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) at 200 kV. Absorption spectra were recorded by the UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cary 100, Agilent Technology, Singapore). Photoluminescent (PL) spectra were measured with F4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (5J2-004, Tokyo, Japan). A Nicolet iS10 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo, USA) was employed to test Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR), geared with an attenuated total reflection (ATR) setup for solid samples. The Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer was used to collect and record X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54178 Å) at a temperature of 25 °C. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the CDs in filtered deionized (DI) water and serum and serum-free Dulbecco’s minimum essential medium (DMEM) were detected by Malvern Zetasizer (Nano-ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). The CDs were suspended in DI H2O at a concentration of 1 mg mL-1, and suspensions were sonicated in a water sonicator bath (KQ-2200 E, Kunshan Instruments Company, China) for 30 min before each use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and treatment with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Mouse HCC Hepa1-6 cells were purchased from the cell bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Cells were grown in DMEM supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and antibiotics at 37 °C, 5% CO2 incubator. Hepa 1–6 cells were cultured until they reached approximately 80% confluency before use. Pharmacological inhibitors were included in this experiment. The autophagy inhibitor 3-methyladenine (3-MA, 1 mM for 2 h) and ROS inhibitor N-Acetyl-cysteine (NAC, 5 μM for 2 h) were respectively exposed to Hepa1-6 cells before the treatment using CDs (100, and 400 μg mL-1) for 12 h.

In order to determine the toxicity of CDs in vitro, the Hepa1-6 cells at the density of 1 × 105 mL-1 (100 μL per well) were distributed into the 96-well plates and exposed to CDs at final concentration of 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400 μg mL-1 for 24 h. Cytotoxicity was determined by a Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) assay (Beyotime Technology, Shanghai, China) and MTT assay (Sigma-Aldrich, USA). Briefly, following the 24 h incubation, cell culture supernatant was collected, centrifuged, and analyzed with LDH assay. After blending the culture medium (50 μL each) and LDH reagent, they were co-cultured in the darkness at room temperature for 30 min and then tested the absorbance at 490 nm by microplate readers (Bio-Tek Instruments, USA). MTT assay was performed by adding 10 μL MTT reagent (5 mg mL-1) to each well, followed by 4 h incubation, after that the supernatants were replaced with 100 μL of dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO). The microplate reader (BioTek Instruments, USA) was applied to measure the absorbance at 570 nm. Moreover, after Hepa1-6 cells incubated with various concentrations of CDs for 24 h, non-exposed and treated cells were washed using PBS and then we surveyed morphological changes under a phase-contrast inverted microscope (Vert.A1, ZEISS, Germany).

To assess the subcellular localization of CDs, Hepa 1–6 cells were seeded on a 35 mm glass-bottom dish overnight, exposed to 100 μg mL-1 CDs and then incubated with 50 nM LysoTracker® Red DND-99 (Beyotime Technology, Shanghai, China) for 4 h at 37 °C. Afterward, cells were washed with PBS for three times and observed using a confocal microscope (FV3000, Olympus, Germany).

All flow data were investigated by flow cytometry (FACS Aria II, Becton Dickinson, San Jose, CA). Hepa 1–6 cells were incubated with 100, and 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 12 h, then washed three times with PBS to remove non-internalized NPs. The cells were collected and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde, the amount of internalized NPs was performed on flow cytometry. The intensities of FSC depend on the size of cells while that of SSC, which can calculate amounts of particles taken up by Hepa1-6 cells is proportional to the intracellular concentration.

Intracellular ROS level was determined by flow cytometry using Reactive Oxygen Species Assay Kit (Beyotime Technology, Shanghai, China). Briefly, after 24 h exposure to CDs of 100, 200 and 400 μg mL-1, Hepa1-6 cells were washed twice with PBS then incubated with DCFH-DA (5 μM) for 30 min at 37 °C. After washed twice with PBS, the fluorescence was observed under a fluorescence microscope, and fluorescence intensities were analyzed by flow cytometry.

The apoptosis in CDs-treated cells was investigated using an Annexin V/PI apoptosis kit (BD Biosciences, USA) by flow cytometry analysis. After 24 h treatment with CDs, Hepa1-6 cells were washed twice with PBS, harvested, and re-suspended with 500 μL of binding buffer. Then 5 μL Annexin V-FITC and 5 μL PI solution were added to cells suspension and incubated for 15 min at room temperature. The stained cells were washed and re-suspended in PBS for flow cytometry analysis.

After incubation on cover glass-bottom dishes to 80% confluence, eGFP-LC3 plasmids were performed with Lipofectamine 2000 (Invitrogen, 11,668, Shanghai, China) according to the manufacture’s protocol. After 24 h transfection, Hepa 1–6 cells were treated with 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 12 h, and then the cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 15 min and stained for nuclei with DAPI (Sigma-Aldrich). The eGFP-LC3 puncta were visualized by confocal microscopy (FV3000, Olympus, Germany).

The lysates are from Hepa 1–6 cells were prepared as previously described (Zhang et al., 2015). The protein concentration of lysates was measured by bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay (Beyotime Institute of Biotechnology, Jiangsu, China). Equal amounts of total protein (20 μg) were loaded onto 13% SDS-PAGE gel and transferred to polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane (Bio-Rad, Hercules, CA). After being blocked with 5% (w/v) non-fat milk in Tris-Buffered-Saline with Tween-20 (TBST) for 1 h at room temperature, the membranes were incubated with primary antibodies against LC3 (Cell Signaling Technology, Danvers, MA, USA), Beclin-1 (Cell Signaling Technology, Danvers, MA, USA), Cleaved caspase-3 (Cell Signaling Technology, Danvers, MA, USA) and the GAPDH (ABclonal, Wuhan, China) overnight at 4 °C, and then incubated with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated anti-rabbit IgG secondary antibodies for 1 h at room temperature. Finally, immunoreactive bands were employed with the SuperSignal West Pico kit (Millipore Corporation, Billerica, MA, USA) and Tanon MP imaging system (Tanon 5200, ShangHai, China), Densitometric values of immunoreactive bands were quantified by ImageJ 1.47 v (National Institutes of Health, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular ultrastructural visualization under transmission electron microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: After treated with 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 12 h, Hepa1-6 cells were harvested, washed with PBS, prefixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde at 4 °C for 1 h, followed by 1% osmium tetroxide treatment for 24 h. After that, cell samples went through the dehydration process in a graded concentration of ethanol and were enclosed by epoxy resin. We contrasted the 40 nm ultra-thin sections with 0.3% lead citrate (Merck, Germany) and imaged them under transmission electron microscopy at 80 kV (JEM1010, JEOL, Peabody, MA).
All data were presented as means ± SD (χ ± SD). Results were statistically analyzed using the Student’s t-test or ANOVA. P values less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

The physical features and chemical properties of the CDs investigated in this study was shown in Fig. 1 . The TEM image suggests that the as-prepared CDs are spherical in shape, non-agglomerated (Fig. 1A) with an average size of 3.06 nm (Fig. 1B). The PL excitation spectra in Fig. 1C showed a single peak at about 332 nm that can be owed to the n–p∗ electronic transition of the aromatic sp2 system with CN and CO bonds in the CDs (Yang et al., 2019). The absorption (black line) and photoluminescence (PL) (gray line) spectra of the CDs were provided in Fig. 1C. The excitation absorption peak of the CDs appeared at 340 nm, with the maximum absorption peak at 440 nm. The FTIR spectra in Fig. 1E showed that through the process of hot-tailoring, the typical peaks at 1600 cm−1 (CN), 1460 cm−1 (CN) and 800 cm−1 (triazine ring sextant out-of-plane bending vibration) of the primitive CN450 precursor were conserved. However, several oxygen-containing groups may have been functionalized on the surfaces of the CDs due to the appearance of some new absorption bands arising from CO (1140 cm−1), CO (1720 cm−1), COH (1252 cm−1), and NH (3418, 3319, 3202 cm−1) (Yang et al., 2019). The hydrodynamic sizes assessed by DLS in H2O, serum-free and serum culture media, showing 3.67 nm, 5.32 nm, and 6.12 nm, respectively. The zeta potentials of CDs indicated −10.07 mV in H2O, −15.44 mV in serum-free media and −12.73 mV in serum culture media.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake, distribution and cytotoxicity of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular uptake of CDs by Hepa1-6 cells was investigated by flow cytometry. In Fig. 2 A, after 12 h treatment of CDs, the SSC intensity increased significantly at 400 μg mL-1, and CDs were internalized in cells in a concentration-dependent manner, indicating the rapid phagocytosis of CDs by liver cells. Intracellular particle localization of CDs upon their uptake was further investigated by co-localization studies, as shown in Fig. 2B, in the confocal images we have seen that the CDs were not free and dispersed in cytoplasm, but rather distinctly localized inside vesicles. Further observations revealed that these vesicles could be stained with LysoTracker Red, indicating that these vesicles represent lysosomes, and after 12 h of CDs treatment, a significant co-localization between CDs and the lysosomal marker were observed. The formation and maturation of acidic organelles initiates with the endocytosis of CDs and carries on with the transfer of internalized CDs from early endosomes to the late ones, and finally to the lysosomes. These results indicated demonstrated that CDs were taken up by Hepa1-6 cells via endocytosis involving endosomes and lysosomes and located within lysosomes, suggesting that CD-cells interactions may involve cellular damage or autophagy clearance in Hepa1-6 cells.

After treatment with CDs at various doses for 24 h, the in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs in Hepa1-6 cells was determined using MTT and LDH assays. Compared with the control group, cell survival inhibition occurred when the CDs dose exceeded 100 μg mL-1, but the treated cells remained almost highly viable (>80% viability) Fig. 2C. Cells treated at doses of 100–400 μg mL-1, similarly, were seen to release more LDH with statistical differences in Fig. 2D. After 24 h CDs exposure, furthermore, morphological variations in cells together with the untreated cells were illustrated in Fig. 2E. The untreated cells (control group) grew well and were closely connected, with increasing doses of CDs (100, 200, 400 μg mL-1), cells were gradually shrinking and getting round while junctions among cells were reducing. Compared to 100, 200 and 400 μg mL-1 treated cells displayed as more toxic with a higher incidence of cell death, with the most apparent morphological features of apoptosis in CDs of 400 μg mL-1 treated Hepa1-6 cells. Thus, our results suggested that CDs were able to reduce the amount of Hepa1-6 cells and increase the cytotoxicity after 24 h treatment, especially at concentrations of 400 μg mL-1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurements of apoptosis in Hepa1-6 cells treated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Apoptosis in Hepa1-6 cells was stained with Annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC)/PI staining and detected by flow cytometry (Fig. 3 A and B). The results showed that the percentage of apoptotic cells increased in CDs of 400 μg mL-1 treated Hepa1-6 cells. Expression of the apoptosis-related proteins BcI-2, Bax, and Cleaved caspase-3 was examined by western blotting analysis. As shown in Fig. 3C, apoptosis in Hepa1-6 cells is caspase-dependent; the level of Cleaved caspase-3 and Bax protein expression increased and anti-apoptotic Bcl-2 protein significant decreases at 24 h after 400 μg mL-1 CDs treatment. These data indicate that apoptosis occurs in CDs-treated Hepa1-6 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ROS production and its relationship with cellular uptake
ce:section.ce:para: ROS as a signaling molecule can activate the antioxidant system to restore homeostasis, and under severe or excessive conditions, ROS switches to pro-apoptosis or results in cell death. Therefore, we were interested to probe whether CDs could induce ROS generation in Hepa1-6 cells. The results demonstrated that intracellular ROS production increased in CDs treated Hepa1-6 cells in a dose- and time-dependent manner (Fig. 4 A). After treatment with 400 μg mL-1 CDs, ROS generation in Hepa1-6 cells was increased to 106% and 230% at 12 and 24 h, respectively. The results indicate that the involvement of ROS may play an important role in cell homeostasis.

It was worth that cellular uptake of NPs was of vital importance in cellular responses including cell multiplication, ROS generation, inflammation, and cell death. Therefore, we further explored the relationship between ROS production and cellular uptake after CDs treatment. According to the histograms of FCM, SSC intensity remained unchanged at the dose of 100 μg mL-1, whereas at a concentration of 400 μg mL-1, SSC intensity reached 230% and DCF fluorescence showed a significant increase after 24 h exposure. Contour plots patterns of SSC and FSC intensity clearly showed the relation between cellular uptake and ROS generation induced by CDs Fig. 4D. In general, a great amount of ROS has been generated by CDs along with a large number of CDs internalized into the cells after 4 h. As shown in Fig. 4D, in untreated cells, the population of low and high SSC intensity approximately 50% overlapped in DCF, whereas after CDs treatment the overlapping areas became smaller and the point to divide low or high SSC intensity slightly moved left and reflected more uptake in cells in a dose-dependent manner. That means high SSC intensity represented the area of high DCF fluorescence of. Thereby, the uptake of abundant CDs leads to large amounts of ROS production and dose was a key factor in the association between uptake and ROS generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs induced autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells and the role of autophagy in CDs-induced toxicity
ce:section.ce:para: To explore whether autophagy is activated in response to CDs treatment. We adopted the cell transfection model of eGFP-LC3 plasmids to assess the occurrence of autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells. The cytoplasmic LC3 (LC3-I) inactive appears diffused throughout the cytoplasm in untreated cells, while activated LC3 (LC3-II) appear as bright punctuates was detected in positive control drug, rapamycin. As shown in 5 A, 400 μg mL-1 CDs could induce eGFP-LC3 puncta formation in Hepa1-6 cells, indicating CDs induce the occurrence of autophagy in Hepa1-6 cells. In addition, the formation of LC3-II puncta was further verified by western immunoblotting assays, the expression of LC3-II increased at 12 h while declining significantly at 24 h. The Beclin 1 was increased significantly after 12 h and remained at a high level at 24 h (Fig. 5 C), indicating that the level of autophagy in Hepa 1–6 cells was significantly increased under CDs treatment. Notably, numerous feature double-membrane based vacuoles formation were observed after treatment with 400 μg mL-1 CDs (Fig. 5F), the TEM images reveal autophagic vacuoles (AVs) morphological features: initial AVs (AVi) contain intact cytosol and/or organelles with a sealed double-membrane bilayer separated by an electron-lucent cleft, whereas late-stage degradative AVs (AVd) after fusion with late endocytic organelles are those containing small internal vesicles and/or organelles at various stages of degradation, electron-dense amorphous material (Tammineni et al., 2017). All of the evidence convincing the autophagy induced by CDs in Hepa 1–6 cells after CDs treatment.

To further verify the role of autophagy in CDs-induced toxicity in Hepa1-6 cells, 3-MA was used to inhibit the cellular autophagic pathway and observed changes in apoptosis and toxicity. As shown in Fig. 6 , the levels of Cleaved caspase-3 and LC3-II expression were significantly increased after pre-treated with of 3-MA (1 mM) for 1 h and incubated with 400 μg mL-1 CDs for 12 h and the percentage of the apoptotic cells were increased from 10.9% to 15.1% (Fig. 6D). These results suggested that autophagy plays an important role in reducing apoptosis. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6E, with 3-MA pre-treatment, cell viability at the concentrations of 200, 400 μg mL-1 decreased significantly. Taken the above together, our current evidence suggested that, the CDs-treated cells performed higher basal autophagy activity as a protective mechanism against the apoptosis effects of CDs in Hepa1-6 cells, and finally result in cells survival.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of ROS generation on CDs triggered autophagy
ce:section.ce:para: ROS is well-known inducer of autophagy, we were quite interested to explore whether ROS generation impacts the autophagy trigged by CDs. Cells were pretreated with NAC, a well-known inhibitor of ROS and examine the formation of green puncta in eGFP-LC3 overexpressing cells. Compared with 400 μg mL-1 of CDs, a significant decrease of eGFP-LC3 punctuates in Hepa1-6 cells was observed after pre-treatment of NAC in Fig. 7 A and B. Further study by western immunoblotting assays demonstrated that after pre-treated with 1 mM of NAC, the expression of LC3-II/LC3-Ⅰ decreased 1.62 folds at 12 h, while Cleaved caspase-3 decreased significantly. All of the above clearly indicated that ROS production could facilitate the formation of autophagy induced by CDs in Hepa1-6 cells. Apart from the above, decreased of Cleaved caspase-3 means a decline of apoptosis. Therefore, ROS production relating to both autophagy and apoptosis is critical in the mechanism of CDs triggered toxicity. To determine the effects of ROS on CDs induced cytotoxicity, we further used NAC, an ROS scavenging agent to verify that ROS was involved in CDs-triggered cell death. With NAC treatment, cell viability at the concentrations of 200, 400 μg mL-1 increased, no potent cell viability variations have been observed for the 100 μg mL-1 dose Fig. 7F. To sum up, our results illustrated that ROS significantly mediated CDs induced autophagy and apoptosis.

Owing to the unique character of emitting fluorescence in the near-infrared (NIR) spectral region, CDs became a very attractive promising material biomedical applications. There is still insufficient information on the potential adverse effects of the various organ tissues for CDs. It has been reported that the liver was a potential accumulation target for CDs through either particle uptake and/or diffusion from primary exposure sites post CDs administration (Mirshafiee et al., 2018). Therefore, hepatotoxicity is one of the major emerging issues concerning the potential hazards of CDs. The reported hepatotoxicity studies in vivo and in vitro on CDs are very limited. In the present work, we systemically assessed the safety of an in-house synthesized CDs in hepatocyte-like Hepa 1–6 cells, which are the most abundant type of cell in the liver and play an important role in building proteins. We found CDs were localized in the lysosome and caused ROS production which correlated with intracellular uptake of CDs. Interestingly, at high concentrations, CDs trigger autophagy, which acts as a protective agent against CDs-induced apoptosis and cytotoxicity. CDs initiate an adaptive stress response which dependent on dose responses to regulate survival and death and ultimately dictates the cellular fate, these findings provide us with valuable insights into the mechanisms by which CDs induces cell death in Hepa1-6 cells, which contribute to the safety evaluation of CDs in a potential target organ, i.e. liver.

In consequence of their small size, CDs can enter easily into cells by endocytosis and then transfer them to the subcellular area like lysosomes. Our results indicated the intracellular uptake of CDs was concentration-dependent. Furthermore, we found CDs showed a high rate of co-localization to endosomes and lysosomes after 12 h of exposure. The cellular internalization process to some degree determines the function of NPs in biomedical application and health hazards. Apart from the observations of favorable physico-chemical properties of the as-synthesized CDs, the aim of this study was to evaluate the biologic toxic effects and fate of our as-synthesized CDs as well. It is worth noting that after small-sized CDs enter the cell, CDs inhibited cell growth and induced a decline in mitochondrial activity at a high level of CDs in Hepa1-6 cells. Only moderate cytotoxicity of CDs observed at the high dose in Hepa1-6 cells with an IC50 value larger than 2000 μg mL-1 for 24 h. Compared with previous studies, our as-prepared CDs showed a favorable biological feature of CDs with lower cytotoxicity. According to Luo, when HT-29 cells and MCF cells exposed to 100 μg mL-1 CDs, cell viability remained approximately 60%, lower than that in this research (Lou et al., 2013). Moreover, Zhang (Zhang et al., 2018) used the CCK-8 assay and found 80–90% A549 cells and HepG2 cells living given the dose of 10 μg mL-1 while we got similar results of cell viability with the dose of 100 μg mL-1. Havrdova (Havrdova et al., 2016) have focused on the effect of surface charge properties on the toxicity of CDs. Their results indicated that using NIH/3T3, neutral CDs-PEG were non-toxic up to concentrations of 300 μg mL-1. In addition, negatively charged CDs-Pri disturbed cell cycle, led to higher oxidative stress whereas the most toxic positively charged CDs-PEI could enter the cell nucleus and triggered the obviously changes in cell cycle even around 100 μg mL-1. From the aspect of toxicity, the as-prepared CDs showed dose-dependent moderate cytotoxicity in Hepa 1–6 cells, and 100 μg mL-1 is probably the threshold dose range for CDs imaging for cells, more suitable for biomedical application. It is worth noting that in this study, CDs do not cause significant toxicity and whether this is achieved by disturbing cell homeostasis requires further exploration.

As we all know, autophagy operates as a highly integrated cellular homeostasis maintenance process, and play an important process in cell living and many diseases. Autophagy is an intracellular degradation process where damaged organelles are collected in the autophagosome, which finally fuses with a lysosome (Levine and Kroemer, 2008). In our study, CDs enter the cells and accumulate in the lysosomes, and AV formation in TEM and an increase in LC3 puncta formation were observed after CDs treatment. The LC3-Ⅱ is known to be significantly involved in the formation of autophagic vesicles, and LC3-II/LC3-I ratio was correlated with the extent of autophagosome formation. The CDs treatment was shown to promote autophagy as evidenced by the expression of Beclin-1 and LC3-II/LC3-I conversion ratio were enhanced. Autophagy is likened to the two Faces of Janus, as it could protect cells to survive certain severe conditions, also induces cell damage when impaired autophagy or too much autophagy occurs (Liu et al., 2011). The induction of autophagy by NPs also acts as a double-edged sword. In order to examine the role of autophagy in cell homeostasis, we used 3-MA, an autophagy inhibition, to pre-treat cells to observe cell viability and apoptosis. After cell autophagy inhibited with 3-MA, cell viability and apoptosis both declined. That indicates that autophagy can help cells survive to some degree, that is to say, autophagy serves as a cellular protective mechanism to maintain homeostasis, engulfs CDs to reduce their cytotoxicity in Hepa 1–6 cells.

Numerous studies have shown that NPs can induce an increasing intracellular ROS. Excessive ROS generation will disrupt the redox homeostasis, lead to the oxidative stress and ROS-mediated damage of organelles and macromolecule as well as affect their structures and disruption signal transductions and cellular dysfunction (Del Rio L A, 2016). A few researches on CDs reported the loss of cell viability caused by ROS production. In yeast cells, growth inhibition might owe to a dose-response ROS generation according to Bagheri’s study (Bagheri et al., 2018). Also, Ronzani (Ronzani et al., 2018) showed the exposure of cells to cationic CDs (3–50 μg mL-1) for 4 h resulted in a dose-dependent increase in ROS production, which is consistent with decreased cell viability. Furthermore, ROS were found to be involved in apoptosis induced by QDs, and apoptosis was attenuated when ROS was inhibited. There were evidence indicated that ROS was involved in the regulation of autophagy, which are the switches of cell survival and death (Filomeni et al., 2015). Therefore, here we further investigated whether CDs can generate ROS in Hepa1-6 cells, which could be responsible for the initiation of autophagy. Our findings observed a significant increase in intracellular ROS under 400 μg mL-1, and ROS generation correlated properly with the onset of intracellular uptake, indicating the CDs uptake may eventually have led to the ROS generation at higher CDs concentrations. Recent research illustrates that autophagy, a highly-regulated intracellular catabolic process for the degradation of damaged organelles, may contribute to the maintenance of cell homeostasis in response to reduce the damage of oxidative stress (Filomeni et al., 2014). Emerging evidence has suggested that the homeostasis disturbance of the oxidative stress in cells by the inhibition of caspase results in the induction of autophagy (Liu et al., 2017), and shown that ROS may be one of the major mediators in the regulation of autophagy, also further to relieve the occurrence of ROS-induced apoptosis. To clarify the role of ROS in autophagy in CDs-induced Hepa1-6 cells, the levels of autophagy and apoptosis were examined after pretreated with NAC. When cells were pretreated ROS scavenger N-acetylcysteine (NAC), the level of eGFP-LC3 puncta were decreased, the results demonstrated that the level of activated autophagy obviously declined. It goes without saying that an obvious decrease of Beclin-1, LC3-II expression were observed following pretreatment with NAC. This clearly suggests that ROS production could facilitate the formation of autophagy induced by CDs in Hepa1-6 cells. Apart from the above, caspase-3 and Cleaved caspase-3, the activation form of caspase-3, both decreased, which means a decline of apoptosis. The induction of autophagy and apoptosis with CDs was enhanced, and cell death was increased in response to the NAC, results of cell viability both increased at the dose of 200 μg mL-1 and 400 μg mL-1. That demonstrated ROS production played a role in the cytotoxicity of Hepa1-6 cells. At the same time, we also noticed CDs-treated Hepa1-6 cells displayed a clear but incomplete reduction of cellular autophagy levels. A possible explanation might be the surface oxygen groups on CDs, which decrease damages from DUV lights. Results indicated that there is no significant sign of cytotoxicity observed at exposure to a very low concentration of 100 μg mL−1 using CDs which was related to the role of autophagy in maintaining cellular homeostasis. Although the dose used as a biomaterial was much lower than the toxic effect does set in this study, the potential impact on the safe use of CDs should not be ignored. In the balance of cellular homeostasis, the role of ROS is necessary to maintain the physiological function of cells, but excessive ROS production is potentially destructive. Previous studies have indicated the close relationship between ROS and autophagy and their role in cell survival or death (Zhang et al., 2017; Mao et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2019). Here autophagy activation was a key factor in maintaining cellular homeostasis and against CDs induced apoptosis. The decreasing of LC3-II proteins and increased cell viability by NAC pretreatment validated that ROS could raise autophagy to support survival in CDs-treated Hepa1-6 cells. Therefore, the interaction between ROS, autophagy, and apoptosis kept a dynamic balance to maintain cellular homeostasis in CDs-treated cells.

There have been numerous reports of the development of biomedical applications of CDs include bio-imaging, drug delivery, and cancer therapy. After intravenous administration, particles with a size range in the 20 nm were easily recognized and captured by the liver. although the part of particles could be excreted through the kidney, some particles within this size range often retained in the liver. Toxicity experiments in vivo also demonstrate that little obvious toxic effects of CDs were observed in treated mice, and though some CDs seen in the liver, spleen, and kidneys, accumulation levels were basically low (Wang et al., 2014). Results of Yang (Yang et al., 2018) showed that CDs led to animals death at higher doses for 30 days and induced lung and liver injury after single inhalation treatment at 5, 2 and 1 mg kg-1. Hong (Hong et al., 2018) also supported CDs that did not trigger any obvious organ impairment but it had changed several physiological and biochemical indexes. Tao (Tao et al., 2012) discovered neither animal death nor vital body weight were changes was observed in female Balb/c mice after exposure to CDs for over 90 days, even at the injected dose of 20 mg kg-1. To date, the in vivo toxicity of CDs has not been comprehensively studied. The very good biocompatibility, as well as the low toxicity of CDs to cells, maybe the main reason for the scarceness of the in vivo toxicity study. Another reason lies in that, CDs may not cause significant damage effects after short-term exposure, but may cause a steady-state imbalance of tissue or cells at the contact site, and may cause liver when chronic contact and massive accumulation in the liver. In our study, CDs were observed to cause significant production of ROS in hepatocytes exposed to high dose levels, cell survival inhibition and apoptosis, autophagy acts as a protective mechanism to remove excess reactive oxygen species and reduce the apoptotic rate. Future studies are warranted to confirm the alteration in the above pathways and their related effects upon CDs-induced liver toxicity in vivo. In addition to hepatocytes (parenchymal cells), the resident macrophage population (Kupffer cells-KCs) with phagocytosis in the liver are also important for assessing the hepatotoxicity of particles. KCs are the largest macrophage, comprising about 80% of the total macrophages of the body. It is very important for the liver to clean up the exogenous matter, especially for identifying and removing particles in the live. Therefore, thus the role of KCs in CDs-induced liver injury should be concerned in evaluating liver toxicity and unraveling pathways of toxic effects as well.

In summary, our as prepared N-doped CDs showed a good fluorescence performance and can enter cells to bio-image and mark organelles. CDs were found to be localized in the lysosome observed and caused ROS production which correlated with intracellular uptake of CDs. We demonstrated ROS generated by CDs was likely an important autophagy initiator and autophagy acts as a protective defender in CDs-induced cytotoxicity, which may be helpful to better understand CDs toxicity. Our findings revealed dose-dependent moderate cytotoxicity and toxicological mechanism in Hepa 1–6 cells, also provides a fundamental theory on the safety evaluation of CDs in a major target organ, i.e. liver, and paved the way for the prevention of CDs-induced hepatotoxicity.

Ting Zhang designed and directed the project. Jing Qu wrote the manuscript with support from Ting Zhang. Ying Yao designed and performed the experiments. Mengran Yang and Yuanjian Zhang manufactured the CDs and characterized them contributed to CDs preparation. Yitenng Zhang, Ying Ma, Daming Wu, and Yuna Cao processed the experimental data, performed the analysis, Meng Tang and Yuepu Pu contributed to the interpretation of the results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author reports no conflicts of interest in this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was Supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81673218), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2242019K40223) and the National College Student Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program (No. 201810286142).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zheng et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Inhibition of kupffer cell autophagy abrogates nanoparticle-induced liver injury

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, S., Zhang, J., Zhang, L., Ma, W., Man, N., Liu, Y., Zhou, W., Lin, J., Wei, P., Jin, P., Zhang, Y., Hu, Y., Gu, E., Lu, X., Yang, Z., Liu, X., Bai, L., Wen, L., 2017. Inhibition of Kupffer Cell Autophagy Abrogates Nanoparticle-Induced Liver Injury. Advanced Healthcare Materials 6, 1601252.
